design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore ancient ruins across the globe, defeat enemies in rooms to gain permanent stat bonuses (jewels), acquire form-specific abilities gated by story progression, solve environmental puzzles unique to each playable character
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Forward momentum vs. exploration completionism—areas cannot be revisited (except late game), creating permanent missability pressure for optional Red Jewels and stat gains
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as a young explorer uncovering world-saving secrets across historical locations, transforming into alternate forms (dark knight Freedan, energy being Shadow) to overcome challenges
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Aggressive anti-grinding design: no XP system, no currency, no equipment shops, scarce healing items, fixed difficulty, and one-way level design that punishes backtracking impulses. Story-gated character abilities mean certain areas are permanently locked behind narrative progression, not player skill. The game trusts players to engage with exploration on first pass or accept failure.

difficulty.lives_system: Will loses all lives when health reaches zero; if player has 100+ life points at death, resume at start of current stage instead of losing outright
difficulty.checkpointing: Dark Spaces serve as save points and healing stations scattered throughout combat and non-combat areas
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Fixed difficulty setting with no adjustable options. Player agency expressed through form selection (Will/Freedan/Shadow for different approaches) and whether to pursue optional stat bonuses before advancing

progression.upgrade_system: Permanent stat increases (attack, defense, health) via jewels dropped after clearing enemy rooms. No equipment system. Character forms (Will → Freedan → Shadow) unlock via story. Each form gains new techniques tied to narrative milestones
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story-gated: new playable forms and their unique abilities unlock as plot advances. Specific areas require specific character forms to enter, creating mandatory story-paced access to locations
replayability_hooks: Red Jewels collection (side quest, cannot be completed if missed before story gates close specific areas)
Enemy re-spawning in previously cleared areas grants health drops but not stat bonuses, enabling farming for life points
Three playable characters with distinct movesets encourage different approach strategies

world_building_techniques: Real-world historical locations (Great Pyramid, Great Wall of China, Angkor Wat, Nazca Lines, Incan ruins) reimagined as fantasy ruins tied to a world-saving narrative
Environmental puzzle design unique to each location reflects its historical/cultural identity
Age of Exploration setting (16th century analogue) provides context for ancient ruin expeditions
Dark Space encounters: surreal safe-spaces where Gaia (and later character statues) deliver exposition and grant abilities
Character-specific dialogue and interactions embedded in towns and villages (e.g., unused text reveals NPCs had deeper interactive roles in development)

boss_design.philosophy: Bosses cannot be revisited and serve as story milestones rather than repeatable challenges. Each appears once, tied to location climax or narrative beat. No documented pattern telegraphing or adaptive AI; defeat is terminal progression
notable_bosses: Dark Gaia (final form of comet, defeated at Tower of Babel climax)

faction_indicators: Will (Light Knight) vs. Freedan (Dark Knight) duality merged with Shadow—narrative framing of light/dark opposing forces
Kara as Light Knight counterpart, suggesting gender/role complementarity in world-saving cosmology
danger_signals: Comet as approaching apocalypse weapon (ancient Blazer War artifact)
World's evolutionary disruption caused by comet's previous approaches
Mandatory erasure of player agency: Will and Kara lose all memories post-victory, suggesting world reset via narrative closure rather than player choice
environmental_storytelling: Ruins of real civilizations carry lore fragments (each holds a puzzle piece for Tower of Babel revelation)
Towns and villages reflect Age of Exploration cultural imprint
Dark Spaces as liminal zones where supernatural meets terrestrial world
Physical journey across globe mirrors real-world exploration paradigm

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor: Illusion of Gaia)
